does: This paper introduces RE-Bench, a benchmark consisting of seven open-ended machine learning research engineering environments designed to evaluate AI agents' capabilities in frontier AI R&D tasks. The benchmark includes empirical data from 71 eight-hour attempts by 61 human experts performing realistic research engineering work. The authors measure both human and AI agent performance across these environments, finding that 82% of expert attempts achieve non-zero scores and 24% meet or exceed strong reference solutions. Using various frontier language models with best-of-k sampling and different time budgets, they directly compare human and AI performance, discovering that the best AI agents achieve approximately 4x higher scores than human experts under a 2-hour time constraint, though humans demonstrate better returns on extended time budgets and narrowly exceed top AI agent performance when given the full 8-hour allocation.
connects: This work addresses a gap identified in frontier AI safety literature, which emphasizes the importance of anticipating AI-driven automation of AI R&D itself but lacks rigorous evaluation frameworks. It builds on prior work in benchmarking AI capabilities through concrete task environments, similar to how previous research evaluated language models on scientific and coding challenges. The paper extends existing agent evaluation methodologies by incorporating human expert baselines rather than relying solely on task completion metrics, drawing inspiration from human-AI comparison studies in domains like game playing and theorem proving. It responds to calls within the AI safety community for more realistic capability assessments that go beyond synthetic benchmarks, connecting concerns about recursive self-improvement and AI automation of research with measurable empirical evaluation.
enables: This benchmark enables future work to track progress in AI R&D automation capabilities, providing a standardized evaluation framework for comparing successive generations of language models and agent designs. The gap between human expert performance at 8 hours and AI performance at 2 hours suggests promising research directions in understanding and improving AI agent sample efficiency and time management. The finding that humans show better returns to extended time budgets points toward investigations of AI agent limitations in long-horizon planning, learning from experience, and strategic resource allocation. RE-Bench supports more informed policy discussions around AI automation risks by grounding them in empirical capability measurements. The benchmark also enables study of which specific research engineering subtasks prove most amenable or resistant to AI automation, informing both capability development and safety considerations.
